title: Observations on the U.S. Economy in a Changing Global Landscape
summary: These are conference opening remarks on heightened global risks and economic fragmentation and their medium-term implications for monetary policy; Collins explicitly states she is not commenting on her near-term policy outlook. Her substantive macro message is structural: a more volatile, fragmented global economy could produce more volatile business cycles and a transitional period of inflationary pressures (reversing the disinflationary effect of pre-pandemic globalization), though robust productivity gains could mitigate this. Because she withholds any read on the current stance or near-term direction, this speech carries little reaction-function signal.

key_quotes.quote: But let me emphasize that these considerations do not affect my near-term policy outlook, which I am not commenting on in these remarks.
key_quotes.context: Explicitly declines to give a near-term policy signal

key_quotes.quote: The shift toward economic fragmentation could do the opposite and usher in a transitional period of inflationary pressures.
First, a more volatile and fragmented global environment could result in more volatile business cycles and inflation.
key_quotes.context: Medium-term implications for the Fed's price-stability mandate
